# biomechanics of prostheses

![Mobile Features AB](https://www.vaia.com/app/themes/studypress-core-theme/dist/assets/images/ab-test/mobile-header.png)

The [biomechanics](/en-us/explanations/medicine/anatomy/biomechanics/) of prostheses involves studying the mechanical principles and forces that influence artificial limbs to ensure functionality, comfort, and efficiency in replicating natural limb movements. By understanding the interaction between [prosthetic components](/en-us/explanations/medicine/dentistry/prosthetic-components/) and the human body, advancements are made in materials and designs to enhance mobility and adapt to each individual's dynamic needs. This field is essential for improving the [quality of life](/en-us/explanations/medicine/palliative-geriatric-care/quality-of-life/) for individuals with limb loss, providing solutions that promote natural movement and reduce fatigue.

## Biomechanics of Prostheses Overview

The field of **[biomechanics](/en-us/explanations/medicine/anatomy/biomechanics/) of prostheses** involves the application of mechanical principles to understand how artificial limbs function and interact with the human body. This area of study is vital for the development of prosthetic limbs that closely mimic the movement and functionality of biological limbs.

### Understanding the Biomechanics of Prostheses

Understanding the biomechanics of prostheses is essential in designing artificial limbs that meet the needs of users, providing comfort, accessibility, and effectiveness. Several factors need to be considered, including:

Mathematics is integral for designing prosthetic limbs. Consider the force calculations for a prosthetic knee during walking:

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter | Formula | Description |
| Torque | \(\tau = F \times d\) | Where \(\tau\) is torque, \(F\) is force, and \(d\) is the distance from pivot. |
| Energy | \(E = \frac{1}{2} m v^2\) | Kinetic energy in motion, \(m\) is mass and \(v\) is velocity. |

Modern prosthetics are not just passive supports; they incorporate technologies such as **myoelectric sensors** that detect muscle signals. These sensors enable the prosthetic to perform more complex movements, such as grasping delicate objects or exerting just the right amount of force.Additionally, researchers are exploring the use of **bionic limbs** that interface directly with the nervous system, offering potential for greater control and sensation feedback.

### Historical Development of Prosthetic Biomechanics

The evolution of prosthetic biomechanics has been driven by a need to enhance [quality of life](/en-us/explanations/medicine/palliative-geriatric-care/quality-of-life/) for amputees. Early prosthetics were simple peg legs and hooks, offering limited function. Advances in materials like carbon fiber and titanium have allowed for prosthetics that are lightweight yet strong.

Historically, significant milestones include:

The understanding of human motion through kinematics and dynamics has also played a crucial role in shaping modern prostheses. For example, equations of motion such as \(F = ma\) are used to simulate the forces a prosthetic limb will encounter, ensuring that they can withstand realistic conditions.

## Biomechanics of Lower Limb Prostheses

The **biomechanics of lower limb prostheses** is a fascinating area that combines engineering, biology, and medicine to aid individuals after amputation. This discipline aims to create prostheses that replicate natural limb behavior as closely as possible through advanced materials and designs.

### Importance in Rehabilitation and Mobility

Lower limb prosthetics play a crucial role in rehabilitation, helping individuals regain mobility and independence. Key contributions include:

From a biomechanics perspective, understanding joint kinematics is critical. For example, the knee joint's flexion involves torque calculations:

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Component | Equation | Description |
| Torque on the knee | \(\tau = r \times F\sin(\theta)\) | Where \(\tau\) is torque, \(r\) is the distance to the joint center, \(F\) is the force, and \(\theta\) is the angle between force and lever arm. |
| Range of Motion | \(\theta\_{max} = 120^{\circ}\) | Maximum flexion angle, typically considered in design. |

Consider an athlete with a lower limb prosthetic designed for high-impact activities. The design incorporates carbon fiber for lightweight strength and a customized mechanical ankle for maximum power transfer during a sprint.

Prosthetics with electronically controlled knees can adapt real-time to various terrains, enhancing usability.

### Challenges and Solutions in Lower Limb Prosthetic Design

Designing lower limb prosthetics poses several challenges due to the complex interplay of forces and materials involved. Some common challenges include:

Solutions may involve:

Innovations in haptics are enhancing the feedback users receive from prosthetics. By integrating sensors that mimic skin receptors, advanced prosthetic limbs can simulate the sensation of touch. Using microprocessors, these systems can adjust grip tightness, provide temperature feedback, and alert users to changes in external conditions.

Future developments in prosthetics include neural interfacing, where prosthetics are controlled directly by neural signals, offering profound improvements in precision and control.

## Biomechanical Principles in Prosthetic Design

In the development of [prosthetic devices](/en-us/explanations/medicine/occupational-therapy-theory/prosthetic-devices/), the **biomechanical principles** are foundational to engineering solutions that offer user reliability and comfort. These principles guide how artificial limbs are engineered to mimic natural anatomical functions, ultimately enhancing the wearer's quality of life.

### Key Principles and Their Applications

Several key biomechanical principles are applied in [prosthetic design](/en-us/explanations/medicine/dentistry/prosthetic-design/):

Mathematical modeling plays a crucial role in these applications. For instance, calculating the forces on a prosthetic foot during gait:

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Variable | Formula | Description |
| Force (F) | \(F = ma\) | Newton's second law, where \(m\) is mass and \(a\) is acceleration. |
| Stress (\(\sigma\)) | \(\sigma = \frac{F}{A}\) | Where \(F\) is force and \(A\) is cross-sectional area. |

An example of biomechanical application is a prosthetic knee equipped with a hydraulic mechanism. This system mimics flexion and extension of a natural knee by controlling fluid flow through valves, adjusting resistance based on walking speed.

Advanced prosthetic designs are now incorporating **smart technology**. These prosthetics can adapt to different environments by using embedded sensors and processors to adjust stiffness and resistance in real-time, offering a more natural movement experience. The integration of artificial intelligence into prosthetic control systems allows for the anticipation of a user's intended motions by analyzing patterns in movement dynamics.

Some prosthetic limbs utilize actuators to create responsive motion, simulating muscle contractions.

### Role of Biomechanics in Prosthetic Limb Functionality

Biomechanics plays a pivotal role in ensuring prosthetic limbs perform effectively and are comfortable for the user. By simulating the natural movement and load-bearing capacity of biological limbs, biomechanics helps in achieving:

Understanding biomechanical forces in prosthetics involves calculations like:

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Component | Equation | Description |
| Moment (M) | \(M = F \times d\) | Where \(F\) is the force applied, and \(d\) is the distance from pivot point. |
| Work (W) | \(W = F \times d \times \cos(\theta)\) | Where \(\theta\) is the angle of force application. |

A prosthetic arm might utilize [electromyography](/en-us/explanations/medicine/history-of-medicine/electromyography/) (EMG) sensors that read electrical activity from contracted muscles, translating these signals into precise movements by motors within the prosthesis.

## Mechanical Properties of Prosthetic Materials

The design of prosthetic limbs relies heavily on the mechanical properties of materials to ensure durability, comfort, and function. Proper material selection influences the overall performance and user experience of [prosthetic devices](/en-us/explanations/medicine/occupational-therapy-theory/prosthetic-devices/), making it a critical aspect of biomechanical engineering.

### Material Selection Criteria

When selecting materials for prosthetic limbs, several key criteria are considered to match functionality with biological tissues:

Calculations for stress and strain are often conducted to test material viability:

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Parameter | Formula | Description |
| Stress (\(\sigma\)) | \(\sigma = \frac{F}{A}\) | Where \(F\) is force applied, and \(A\) is the cross-sectional area. |
| Strain (\(\epsilon\)) | \(\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L\_0}\) | Where \(\Delta L\) is change in length, \(L\_0\) is original length. |

**[Biocompatibility](/en-us/explanations/medicine/dentistry/biocompatibility/)**: A characteristic of being compatible with living tissue or a living system by not being toxic, injurious, or physiologically reactive, and not causing immunological rejection.

Materials like thermoplastics can be easily molded when heated, offering versatility in initial shaping and future adjustments.

### Advances in Prosthetic Material Technologies

Recent technological advances have further refined the materials used in prosthetic devices, bringing enhancements in performance and realism:

Applications of advanced materials often involve complex calculations, such as estimating energy absorption to improve shock absorption:

|  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Energy (\(E\)) | \(E = \frac{1}{2}mv^2\) | Where \(m\) is mass, \(v\) is velocity; relevant in designing shock-absorbing components. |

An example of cutting-edge material application is the use of graphene, known for its remarkable strength and lightweight, in creating prosthetics that offer superior performance while minimizing user load.

Biomedical research is exploring the integration of conductive polymers in prosthetics, providing electronic pathways within the limb for sensors and feedback mechanisms. Such technologies aim to develop advanced interfaces that can not only move but also feel, bridging the gap between prosthetic and live tissue functionality.

## Flashcards in biomechanics of prostheses

Maintaining a uniform shape for all users regardless of need.

What equation is used to calculate the torque on a prosthetic knee joint?

\(\tau = 2\pi F r\)

Which material property is essential for simulating natural limb movement?

Strength using carbon fiber.

How is torque calculated in prosthetics?

Applying \(E = mc^2\) for energy equivalence.

What is essential for designing effective prosthetic limbs?

Ignoring mechanical principles for cost efficiency.

What technology allows for custom manufacturing of prosthetics at reduced costs?

3D Printing Technologies.

--- SOURCE 3: An Introduction to the Biomechanics of Prosthetics ---
URL: https://www.azorobotics.com/Article.aspx?ArticleID=11

SUMMARY:
A prosthetic limb is defined as a mechanical device that is used to replace a missing human limb. The device is designed to help the user

FULL CONTENT:
![AZoRobotics](https://www.azorobotics.com/themes/Standard/images/azorobotics_logo_v3.png)
![An AZoNetwork Site](https://www.azorobotics.com/images/an-azonetwork-site.svg)

# An Introduction to the Biomechanics of Prosthetics

*This article was updated on the 12th September 2019.*

![](https://www.azorobotics.com/images/Article_Images/ImageForArticle_11(1).jpg)

![](https://www.azorobotics.com/images/Article_Images/ImageForArticle_11(1).jpg)

*Andrii Zastrozhnov / Shutterstock*

**A prosthetic limb is defined as a mechanical device that is used to replace a missing human limb. The device is designed to help the user coordinate better control of an amputated limb. This could be as a result of motor control loss by a traumatic event, a congenital-related defect, or dyvascular-related.**

According to statistics by Ziegler-Graham, et al. (2008), an estimated 1.6 million civilians were living with the loss of a limb. The research also revealed that approximately 38% of these people suffered an amputation of a limb as a secondary consequence to a dyvascular disease. Shockingly, it has been predicted that this statistic is likely to double to 3.6 million by 2050.

Dyvascular disease is only one major contributing factor to the amputation of a limb. It is commonly known that war veterans often become wounded in combat and considering the current affairs, it is estimated that the number of veterans wounded in Afghanistan is increasing. According to the Department of Defense statistics, to-date more than 1000 soldiers have returned from Afghanistan with the loss of a limb. With current limb-loss statistics in perspective, there is a clear demand for the use of prosthetics from the healthcare angle.

## Robotic Prosthetics - Structural Components

A robotic prosthetic limb is made up of the following components:

The study of prosthetic limbs is vast and complex. For the scope of this article, attention will focus on the prosthetic hand in more detail. As mentioned, the main pathway involved in the function of a prosthetic limb is based on three main components: a biosensor, controller, and actuator. User preferences need to be considered before designing a prosthetic limb and if focusing on a hand prosthesis, the following preferences are important:

The use of biosensors is fundamental to initiating a pathway that will result in movement of the actuator. To allow for the control of a prosthetic limb, the actuators are attached to the residual part of an amputated area, which will provide feedback on tactile information generated by the biosensors. The actuators are also connected to a hardware interface system that acts as the controller, which initiates sensory feedback to the actuators.

## The Actuator

The actuator is the key element to a fully functioning prosthetic hand and it is what displays the end result (i.e., grasping an object). To begin with, the actuator system to this prosthetic hand is made up of micro-actuators. These drive the metacarpo-phalangeal (MP) joints of the thumb and the proximal interphalangeal (PI) joints. The distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint is controlled by a link connected to the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) finger joint.

It is important to consider the number of individual pieces of information that will predict how a certain parameter will behave (degrees of freedom [DOF]). With this in mind, it is known that each finger to a hand has 4 DOF and the wrist has 3 DOF, which means that a prosthetic hand has to be designed to function with 23 DOF. It is quite difficult to control the movement of such a complex machine and so a basic two to three fingered prosthetic hand is usually designed for better control over the monitoring of signal patterns. The thumb actuator works with two degrees of freedom (DOF), which are comparative to the MP and IP joints. The modeling of heavy actuators results in a lower DOF, which can affect the ability of the prosthetic hand to grasp objects effectively. The following video is a good example of a patient adapting a myoelectric prosthetic hand controller with signal activity being monitored.

### Related Stories

To take this journey of prosthetic hand construction a little further into detail, we need to consider the application of the sensor-controller-actuator system when measuring micro-electric signals. An electromyography (EMG) signal is used to detect electrical stimulus from the robotic hand, as would have been the case if the real limb was present. Electromyography involves the use of surface electrodes to monitor muscle activity and when considering bionic signals, this method of signal control is ideal to monitor exactly how the mechanical device is moving and responding to stimuli. The EMG is particularly important as it provides the functionality to amputees and the basic principle of EMG signals is to pick up a signal based on the movement of a limb or robotic form. The video below demonstrates the use of a robotic hand with one degree of freedom to pick up an object and how EMG is used to map the electrical signals during this movement.

## Research to Consider

The present article has introduced a basic overview of the design and modeling of a prosthetic hand, a study referred to as biomechatronic design. Though the design of prosthetics is continuing to develop and benefits many patients living with an amputated limb, there are still challenges ahead in the design of a prosthetic limb that satisfies intricate requirements, such as easy control of the prosthetic limb and a to make this mechanical device cosmetically appealing. There is also the challenge of understanding the issue of tissue reactions to material used for the prosthetic limb and how an inflammatory response to such a reaction may interfere with the signal transmission of biosensors.

## Recent Developments

A lot of work has gone into further understanding and replicating the movements of a human limb to better design robotic hands that can perform as well as or better than human hands. Analysis of available prosthetic hands using computational approaches, revealed that symmetrical designs preferred by roboticists or the random designs generated come close to the abilities of a human hand. Thus, prosthesis designed with anatomically plausible asymmetry in joints and tendons can perform even better than human hands.

Another approach is to replicate the biomechanics of a human hand using biomimetic, mechanical design. The hands can be controlled via methods such as using electromyography signals from the residual limbs or by implanting tiny electrodes in the motor region of the brain or nerves responsible for controlling the hand. To control the movement of the fingers, new approaches have used cable-driven systems where strings replicate the tendons of the hand rather than conventional mechanical joints and transmission systems. This enabled the easier control of fingers as information about movement could be directly passed on to the prosthetic, rather than using complicated [control systems](/Article.aspx?ArticleID=188).

The latest designs combine [machine learning](/Article.aspx?ArticleID=703) and neural networks to enable a user to simply think of a movement and the prosthetic performs it, just like a natural limb. A recent study reported a prosthetic hand combined with a neural network that can allow the wearer to think and move the hand. Electrical signals from the nerves are measured through the skin and sent to a computer that decides what the movement should be. The computer learns what the movement should be using a neural network trained to recognize different movements.

Another aspect of research into prosthesis has been enabling a prosthetic limb to feel. One report used sensors in the fingers to send signals to the nerves of the limb via an array of microelectrodes and wires that translate into a sensation. They may also be used to feel pain and temperature. Another approach has been to use a layer of artificial skin with sensors that are placed over the fingers on a prosthetic hand. The sensors relay the stimuli back to the nerve endings of the limb. Such a system can be used with any type of prosthetic hand.

Although there have been significant developments in prosthetics in the last few years, there is still a need for cheaper and more accessible prosthetics. More prosthetics are moving out of lab-scale testing and into the real world, opening up new opportunities and challenges.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Biomechanics applies mechanical principles to analyze and improve the design, function, and performance of prosthetic limbs, ensuring they replicate natural movement effectively.
Understanding biomechanics is crucial for creating prosthetics that enhance mobility, comfort, and usability, enabling users to perform daily activities with greater ease and efficiency.
Biomechanics informs rehabilitation strategies by analyzing gait and movement patterns, allowing tailored interventions that optimize recovery and improve the quality of life for amputees.
